sentence_body: Chapter Text Cover by azasya, thank you so much ♡ POV Hermione “Hermione, dear, would you mind helping me set the table?
Molly was moving from pot to pot, as fidgety as ever, whenever she was hosting everyone at the Burrow.
“Of course, Molly”, Hermione said, and she headed toward one of the cupboards to take out some plates.
Hermione loved Sundays.
Being with the Weasleys and Harry truly gave her the feeling of having a family.
After the war, she had gone to Australia and managed to restore her parents’ memories.
It had been a difficult time for Hermione, because after a few arguments, her parents had refused to return to London.
They had grown used to the climate and had settled comfortably into their new dentist’s office.
Hermione hadn’t had the heart to fight them, she understood that her parents might still feel resentment towards her.
So eventually she had gone back to London alone and spent the evening crying in Ron’s arms.
Far from her parents, she had only the Weasleys and Potter to hold on to.
“Oh, Molly”, Gabrielle exclaimed, stealing a piece of carrot from the salad bowl, “I just love your shepherd’s pie -it smells absolutely divine!
” “And your chocolate cake”, Fleur added as she picked up the glasses, “it’s the only thing I look forward to on Sunday” “I could literally die for Mum’s cake”, Ginny sighed.
“Hey Hermione, pass me the plates.
Can you bring the pumpkin juice?
” Hermione nodded and handed the plates to Ginny.
Her stomach growled – she was already salivating at the thought of the meal waiting for them.
Ginny nearly tripped and set the stack of plates down on the table.
“James, we’re about to eat.
How about helping me bring the plates in?
I’ll talk to Victoire afterward, alright?
” “Mmh-mh”, he mumbled, pouting as he took the two plates his mother handed him.
James carefully carried them, the tip of his tongue sticking out as he focused hard on his task.
“Ginny!
Lily needs changing!
” George called as he entered, Lily screaming in his arms.
“For Merlin’s sake, where’s her father?
” the readhead groaned, following George out of the kitchen.
Hermione smiled, then get back to preparing the pitchers.
“Hermione, do you think you could watch Louis on Wednesday?
” Gabrielle asked, coming up beside her at the counter.
“Wednesday, you say?
” Hermione thought for a moment as she set down the drinks, and the plates Ginny had left on a tray.
“I think I’m free, I don’t have anything planned.
In the evening?
” Gabrielle nodded, crunching on the carrot she was nibbling “My friends are coming to visit London this week!
I wanted to spend some time with them.
” “Alright, I’ll tell Fleur to drop Louis off at my places,” Hermione replied with a smile.
“You’re such a trésor!
” Gabrielle exclaimed, her French accent growing stronger on the word ‘trésor’.
She ran out of the kitchen to go tell her sister.
Hermione carried the two pitchers to the large table and finished setting it with Ginny’s help.
When they all gathered around the table, the usual hubbub filled the room.
Everyone was talking at once.
The little Potters and Weasleys were so loud that the adults had to raise their voices just to be heard.
Even Louis was trying to join in, imitating his sisters while Fleur attempted to spoon some soup into his mouth.
He had just turned one, but he was already quite alert for his age.
His first accidental magic had happened when he was not even six months old — at the Burrow, no less — when he had sent his yogurt flying onto Arthur’s head.
Lily was fast asleep after Ginny had changed her — well, after she had made Harry do it, by giving him one of her looks — and now she was busy feeding Albus his soup.
“Dominique!
Stop playing with your mashed potatoes!
” Fleur cried.
“And you, Victoire, give your sister back her napkin,” Bill ordered, exasperated.
“She’s the oldest but she’s such a big baby,” James remarked.
“Mum, did you hear what he said?
” “If you keep behaving like this, we won’t come to Grandma’s anymore,” Ginny said sharply, tapping the table.
It was like this every Sunday, but Hermione never grew tired of it.
She was already looking forward to babysitting Louis later that week.
She had looked after Victoire and Dominique before, but it was usually the Potters she watched.
The Weasley-Delacours spent much of the week with Fleur’s family, so it was rare for Hermione and Ron to have them.
Still, she adored this new generation and didn’t mind at all.
“Louis might be spending the evening with us on Wednesday,” she whispered to Ron, who was sitting beside her.
“Wednesday?
” he said, his mouth full.
“But Hermione, I’ve got Quidditch practice on Wednesday night.
You’ll have to stay with him on your own.
” “I knew I was forgetting something,” she exclaimed, rolling her eyes.
“You’ve been really scatterbrained lately,” Ron said after taking a sip of pumpkin juice.
Hermione sighed and picked up a piece of bread to go with her meal.
“My schedule’s packed – I’ve got so much on my mind…” “Hey Ron, what’s the name of that ice-cream shop in Diagon Alley again?
” Gabrielle interrupted, who was sitting across from him.
“It’s Florian Fortescue’s,” Hermione answered for him, since Ron’s mouth was still full.
“You’re going there with your friends, right?
” “Exactly,” Gabrielle said.
“I want to show them the best wizarding shops and make them jealous!
” “But isn’t everything fancier in France?
” Ginny asked, pouting playfully.
“Everything is fancier in France,” Fleur chimed in – and Louis promptly grabbed a handful of her hair.
“As if I’d asked for her opinion,” the readhead muttered in Hermione’s ear.
The week had felt very long for Hermione.
She was tired, and spending the evening looking after Louis had been anything but restful.
She adored Bill and Fleur’s children – that wasn’t the issue.
But taking care of him on her own, when she was used to having Ron with her during babysitting, was something else entirely.
Ron had come home late that night; Bill had picked up the little one long before, and Hermione hadn’t even heard Ron come in.
Her work at the Ministry took up a lot of her time, but she did her best to keep her professional and personal lives separate – and she thought she was managing fairly well.
Ron, however, didn’t seem to notice her efforts.
He spent most of his time playing Quidditch with his friends and sometimes went to help George at his joke shop.
Hermione couldn’t even remember the last time they’d gone out to a restaurant, just the two of them.
But that was adult life – there was rarely any time.
That's the reason why, Hermione felt a bit anxious at the thought of having a child if they ever decided to take that step.
She truly wanted one; for her, it was the ultimate goal of her life with Ron.
She had already talked to him about it, but he always said it was better to live one day at a time, and that children would come when they were ready.
Now, Hermione was slumped over her desk, unable to read a single word properly on the parchment she was holding.
There were new restrictions on the trade of spotted Mackled Malaclaw's tails as potion ingredients.
The Department for the Regulation and Control of Magical Creatures wanted to protect and monitor the population of Malaclaws, now endangered due to the rise in poaching and trafficking.
Hermione would soon have to travel to Brighton to make sure all the regulations were being followed.
Her eyes fell on the small, framed photo on her desk, and she couldn’t help but smile.
It was a picture of her and Ron after one of his Quidditch matches, one he had won.
Ron was grinning from ear to ear, giving a thumbs-up to the camera, while Hermione looked at him lovingly – and the movement looped over and over again.
She rubbed her eyes and decided to go home early.
Ron had said he would stop by George’s shop later that afternoon, and Hermione thought it would be nice to surprise him – to welcome him home with a good homemade dinner.
Yes, he’d surely like that.
“Susan, I’m heading out a bit early today,” Hermione announced to her colleague as she tucked one of her files into her bag.
“No problem,” Susan replied with a smile.
“Planning something with Ron?
” “I’d like to surprise him, yes,” Hermione said, blushing slightly.
Susan smiled and gave her a playful wink.
“Have a lovely evening, Mrs.Weasley,” she said with a teasing tone.
Hermione waved goodbye and apparated directly in front of her house.
Once inside, she slipped off her heels with a sigh of relief.
She was unbuttoning her taupe-coloured jacket when she heard a dull noise coming from upstairs.
Instinct took over – she grabbed her wand, fully alert.
Another sound followed, and her heart started to pound in her chest.
Could Ron have come home early?
She cast a Silencing Charm on her feet and climbed the stairs, her breath quick and shallow.
She heard voices.
They were being robbed – that had to be it.
She knew they should have put up magical wards, but Ron always said she was too paranoid, after the war was over.
She swallowed hard and went over in her mind the spells she was ready to cast.
The noises were coming from the bedroom, and the door was half-open.
She moved forward, still as quietly as ever, and looked inside.
Only his upper body was visible.
The rest, under the blanket, suggested the thrusts he was making, which made the person holding him tightly by the neck moan.
“Don't stop, Ron,” she moaned.
Ron didn't stop.
He picked up the pace, sighing.
This man, whom she knew inside out and who knew her inside out, was sleeping with a woman who was not her.
Hermione didn't realise her hands were shaking.
She couldn't look away.
Very quickly, the sounds of their moans no longer reached her ears.
She couldn't hear anything anymore and everything became blurry.
She didn't understand what was happening to her.
A cry of pleasure brought her back to earth.
“Ron, faster,” she cried.
“Gab, you're going to..
. me..
” he muttered, breathless.
Gab?
Hermione recoiled in horror.
With one hand over her mouth, she ran down the stairs, grabbed her bag and hurried out.
Her knees buckled on the landing and her vision blurred as she tried to get oxygen into her lungs.
Her hand, still over her mouth, stifled her moans of pain, and she clutched her wand to her chest.
Lost between denial and confusion, she Apparated to the first place that came to mind: Harry and Ginny's house.
“Hermione?
” She saw Ginny crouching in front of her, her face filled with concern.
But Hermione couldn't form any words.
The pain in her chest was so dull that she couldn't think of anything else.
It was as if something had broken inside her, everything was spinning around her and weighing heavily on her.
She suddenly stood up and rushed to the bathroom to vomit.
She felt a hand stroking her back.
“She just arrived like that, you say?
” “Do you think we should tell Ron?
” Hermione burst into tears and turned to Harry and Ginny.
Not him.
Not Ron.
She shook her head.
She didn't want to see him.
She needed to think, to get her thoughts in order so she could see more clearly.
She felt nauseous again and leaned over the sink.
Ginny whispered things to her that she couldn't make out.
It was probably her hand she felt on her back.
When her stomach was empty, she leaned back against the wall, breathless.
She looked up at Harry when he reappeared in her field of vision, James at his side.
“Mum, what's wrong with Mione?
” James asked, looking very worried.
“She is a bit sick”, Ginny said, with a sweet voice.
“Go play, Dad will take you to Granny's soon.
” “I sent a Patronus to Ron to warn him.
He should be here soon,” Harry said, crouching down beside Ginny.
Hermione turned pale and shook her head again.
“Please..
. don't call him,” she begged.
Her voice caught in her throat, and Ginny took her face in her hands and wiped her tears away with her thumbs.
“I don't want to see Ron,” she whispered so softly that Ginny didn't seem to understand at first.
But something flashed across her eyes.
She stared at Hermione, her eyebrows furrowed.
“Harry.
Tell Ron that Hermione is ill and that it's best if she stays here because she's too contagious,” Ginny said, without taking her eyes off her.
“What?
” “Go on, Harry.
And drop James and Albus off at Mum's.” His footsteps receded and Ginny helped Hermione to her feet.
She led her to their guest room and settled her into bed.
“What did he do?
” Ginny asked gravely.
Hermione looked up and tried to form a coherent sentence.
Had she been dreaming?
Had what she saw not been a figment of her imagination?
How long had this been going on?
Was this the first time Ron had..
A chill of humiliation ran through her.
He, whom she had kissed every night for years, whom she cherished, whom she loved more than anything in the world..
. had dared to do this to her?
The scene replayed in her mind over and over again.
She felt betrayed.
How could he?
She stifled a sob and Ginny held her close.
She cried for a long time, silently.
And Ginny did not leave her side, not even for a minute.
Hermione must have fallen asleep, because she woke up in complete darkness.
A wave of anxiety filled her stomach as she looked around, trying to remember where she was.
Then she noticed Ginny, asleep beside her, and her heartbeat slowly calmed.
Lily was sleeping in her crib, right next to Ginny.
Hermione grabbed her wand and cast a Tempus.
It was past midnight.
Staring blankly, she saw again the image of Ron with that other girl – Gab.
The sickness hit her instantly.
Ron was cheating on her with Gabrielle.
Hermione knew it deep down; there was no other “Gab” she could think of.
And judging by the intensity of what she’d seen, they were clearly used to being together.
How long had Ron been playing this double game?
And Gabrielle?
She replayed every little detail in her mind.
The Sunday lunches.
Gabrielle always sitting across from Ron.
The way she interrupted him just to ask some trivial question.
And Ron – all those Quidditch evenings… were they really Quidditch evenings?
Hermione had never gone with him, and Ron knew she wasn’t interested enough in Quidditch to ask questions.
She had trusted him blindly.
Ginny stirred beside her and sat up.
She cast a Lumos and set her wand on the bed.
The faint light illuminated the room, and Hermione could see the worried look on her friend’s face.
“Hermione, are you okay?
” Ginny whispered.
Hermione sighed.
She pushed her curls back from her face, and tears welled up in her eyes once again.
“Ginny, what am I going to do?
” “What did he do?
” Ginny took one of her hands and held it tightly between her own.
“You know you can tell me anything.
” Hermione nodded, but she couldn’t stop the tears from falling.
“I came home from work early,” she whispered through sobs.
"I heard noises upstairs and…" Ginny tightened her fingers around hers.
Hermione looked up at her and felt terribly sorry.
How could she tell her sister?
“Take your time, Hermione.
” She sniffed and stared at the wall, distraught.
“I went upstairs and saw him in our bed with another girl.
” Ginny's breath caught in her throat.
They didn't speak for several minutes, and only Hermione's sobs echoed in the room.
“Ginny,” she said, her heart aching.
“It was Gabrielle.
” POV Draco “You’re out of Firewhisky?
” “If I’m out, it’s because you drank it all, Nott.
And get your head out of my cupboard, we’re waiting for you to play.
” “I can’t play the next round with an empty glass.
It’s bad luck.
Don’t you even have a small stash of Elfish wine?
At least some mead?
” “There’s some Butterbeer in the fridge.
And I think there’s a bit of brandy left on the door.
Now get your ass over here so we can play!
” “Brandy… in the fridge…
you sound like a Muggle-born.
You’re going soft, Malfoy", Nott sneered, grabbing the said bottle of brandy while levitating a clean glass toward the card table.
“Says the guy in the middle of a poker game…” the third player muttered at the end of the table without looking up from his cards.
“Sod off, Zabini”, Theodore Nott shot back, pulling the cork out of his prize with his teeth.
“And what about me?
Am I supposed to dry up over here?
” a somewhat high-pitched voice came from the opposite end of the table, as their dealer flipped three cards onto the felt.
“Pass the Muggle potion, Nott.
” “You have a wand, Parkinson”, Draco Malfoy cut in with a quiet chuckle, eyeing his hand and the cards turned over in front of him.
“And you know where the glasses are.
” Pansy Parkinson stuck her tongue out at him in a childish gesture before summoning a small, finely cut crystal glass from the cabinet that stood in the corner of the dining room.
Draco pretended not to notice but couldn’t suppress a faint smile.
He particularly enjoyed their weekly ritual evenings, which always brought them back together in the middle of their otherwise very different weeks.

While Blaise lived comfortably off the inheritance left by his mother and her many widowhoods, that didn’t stop him from managing his wealth with masterful precision a true businessman through and through.
As for the other three, they had made the choice to refuse to live off the riches and fortunes of their families, tainted as they were by their parents’ deeds.
Their reputations had made things rather complicated for them, but they were finally beginning to see the light at the end of the tunnel.

Pansy, after being turned down for countless positions in various prestigious fields, had been forced to face the facts, swallow her pride, and accept the job offered by the editor-in-chief of Witch Weekly as a proofreader (or “scribbler,” as she liked to call herself).
She loathed the office work, but it had allowed her to leave the family manor for a somewhat cramped yet comfortable flat not far from Diagon Alley.
She had learned to keep a low profile, got along well with her team and supervisors, and hoped she might eventually climb the ladder within the magazine if she kept proving herself.
Theo, for his part, had heard about a small new craft brewery launched sometime after the war by a pair of former Gryffindors from their year, Seamus Finnigan and Dean Thomas.

Having had no quarrel with them back at school — a rare enough occurrence for a Slytherin to be worth mentioning — he had boldly shown up one morning to offer his services as head of administrative and financial management.
After tough negotiations, he was taken on a trial basis, and later officially hired when Dean and Seamus saw how well their growing business was being run.
Since then, business had thrived, and Theo was fully satisfied with his career choice.
He had, however, made the wise decision to draw a clear line between work and friendship, never mentioning his two Gryffindor employers to his Slytherin friends or the other way around.
Old grudges ran deep on both sides, and it was best not to stir up the ghosts of the past.
As for Draco, his life was only just beginning to find some balance again.
The first years after the war had been more than difficult for him and his family.
The trial had been long and humiliating, even if it ultimately turned in their favor — thanks to Potter’s testimony.
Potter, in his infinite Chosen One benevolence, had reached out to his mother, Narcissa, after the death of Lucius, which had occurred five years after the war.
Draco had only learned about their meeting afterwards, from his mother herself.
And the matter was rather straightforward, all things considered.
Potter had spent the last few years living in the Noble House of Black, at Grimmauld Place, which he had inherited from his godfather, Sirius Black — the last direct heir of the family.

But he could no longer bear living surrounded by the memories of that man and of all the dark times they had endured in that house, which he could never make feel less grim — not to mention the eternal problem of the old, half-mad aunt’s portrait, forever shrieking on the wall and impossible to remove.

Potter could never have borne the thought of Lucius living in that house, but after learning of his death, it had seemed only natural to him to return the ancestral home to Narcissa, so she could do with it as she pleased.
He had never truly felt entitled to own it and was far more comfortable handing it back to a branch of the original family.
The opportunity was too good to pass up.
Narcissa had long been considering leaving the gloomy Malfoy Manor, where too many dreadful events had taken place before their eyes during the war.
With her husband gone, nothing tied her any longer to the building she now despised.
She had therefore accepted Potter’s offer with genuine enthusiasm and had been living at Grimmauld Place ever since.
She had set about renovating the place with meticulous care — a task far easier for her than it had been for Potter, as she had known the house in its days of splendor.
From the moment she had moved in, the old witch in the portrait had fallen silent, seemingly soothed and satisfied to see a true Black daughter once more inhabiting the ancestral home.
Narcissa had also taken back Kreacher, the old house-elf, into her service.
Saint Potter, of course, would have felt too guilty to tear him away from the house he had served all his life.
From what Draco knew, Potter no longer lived in London.

He had begun restoring his parents’ old home in Godric’s Hollow, where he planned to settle down with the family he had started with his redheaded wife — who, judging by her genes, would no doubt provide him with an entire litter of little weasels.
It hadn’t taken long for Draco to leave the family manor himself.
He felt suffocated there — by the memory of his father, by the weight of dark, painful recollections from the time when the Dark Lord had lived under their roof.

For several months, while deciding what to do with what remained of his life, he had alternated between staying at Zabini Manor and Nott Manor, the latter of which Theo had begun to restore to erase all traces of his patriarch.
Theo had even started by donating most of his inheritance to various charities that aided victims of the war.
The wizarding world had become almost hermetically close to Draco.
His father’s actions, though officially forgiven in the eyes of the law, were far from absolved in the eyes of public morality or collective memory.
He himself had gone too far in his own deeds to be easily forgiven for the mistakes of his youth.
Finding work within the British wizarding world had been unthinkable, and he could not bring himself to settle in a wizarding neighborhood teeming with neighbors who would never welcome him.
It had taken him quite some time to think things through before reaching the conclusion that the best solution was to distance himself from his peers — at least for a while.
Three years earlier, he had finally moved into the spacious cottage he now occupied, on the outskirts of the semi-wizarding village of Upper Flagley.

Here, he could socialize with Muggles without fearing their judgment, and the wizarding families who had been living peacefully among them since the signing of the International Statute of Secrecy in 1689 didn’t concern themselves with his presence so long as he didn’t cause any sort of disturbance that might endanger that delicate balance.
Over time, he had grown accustomed to the slower pace of life in that Yorkshire village.

He had been forced to learn to handle a few essential Muggle contraptions necessary for life without a house-elf — which, obviously, he couldn’t very well summon to a half-Muggle settlement — such as the refrigerator and the washing machine.

Mastering those devilish devices hadn’t been easy, but after several failed attempts and several rather ridiculous incidents he wouldn’t admit to even under Veritaserum, he had to concede they were quite useful.
Still, he had held on to his wizarding habits.
So, although his home was connected to electricity and running water, he had refused to install the pheletone — or was it letephone?
He could never remember the name of that gadget and instead had his fireplace linked to the Floo Network, far more practical in his opinion.

His property was also equipped with every protective charm a man could need shielding and intrusion alerts, and even a Muggle-Repelling Charm on the house itself, just in case some overly curious neighbor ever got the absurd idea to knock on his door.
His latest Muggle acquisition was an electric device meant to make coffee, though he hadn’t yet figured out exactly how it worked.
Muggles, it seemed, were incapable of writing clear and concise instruction manuals, and the unmoving little pictures were absolutely no help.
His brand-new “coffee machine” therefore sat unused on his kitchen counter, gleaming and useless, while he continued to get his caffeine fix at the local café.
“Well ?
Are you going to play or not?
At this rate, the bottle will be empty before I strip you of every Galleon you’ve got and I’ve no intention of switching to your bloody Butterbeer.
” Theo’s voice, far too loud under the influence of alcohol, yanked him out of his reverie.
Draco glanced back at his cards and the pile of bets on the table, then let out a deep sigh before muttering: “I fold.
” The other three burst into noisy protests and a flurry of teasing about his supposed cowardice, laced with more than a few indecent jokes.

Pansy mocked him relentlessly for the rest of the evening, Theo drained the last drop of brandy — earning himself a string of insults from the thirsty croupier — and in the end, it was Blaise who walked away with all their money.
Still, Draco went to bed that night a content man, quietly pleased with the calm life he was starting to build for himself in that small corner of England, far from the ghosts of his past.
That was before the thunderclap — or something very much like one — that jolted him upright in bed the next morning, heart pounding madly.
He was on his feet in an instant, rushing to the window.
There he froze, utterly speechless, taking a few seconds to come to his senses.
When he finally regained full awareness — and the ability to speak — he pulled the curtain aside and shouted, with genuine outrage: “WHAT THE HELL IS THIS?
